posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
These bottles just landed last month and I popped them tonight.

  • 2019 Domaine Jean-Claude Bachelet et Fils Chassagne-Montrachet Rouge Concis des Champs - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Chassagne-Montrachet (12/8/2022)
    PnP. Deep and dark. Surprised to see such depth from a CM red even in this vintage. There's good material here and nice length. This is a serious wine, but really enjoyable with some air thanks to the terroir. Could become something special in a couple or three years. (91 points)
  • 2017 Dureuil-Janthial Rully - France, Burgundy, Côte Chalonnaise, Rully (12/8/2022)
    PnP. Very drinkable. Not too much depth, but nice fruit. My first Rully. Has some unique herbal notes, probably due to the terroir. Surprisingly ripe for the vintage. (88 points)

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Well the CM is in a generally cooler area, traditionally better suited for white growing. So I’ve found them earlier drinking especially in the riper years we’ve had recently.

The Rully, I don’t know. That was my assumption based on never having a bottle from that area before.

posts.name: Vlad B3rk0vsky
posts.cooked: Agree with the notes for 2017 Dureuil-Janthial Rully. Found it simple but pleasant.

Andrew_K:

But certainly not known for really good reds like whites.

Clos St Jean was rated the same as vineyards like beaux monts and echezeaux in the 1865 assay. Wine hog has a good article on it.

Winehog - with a passion – 26 Oct 15

Clos Saint-Jean ... the red Grand Cru of Chassagne-Montrachet?

I have always enjoyed a good glass of red Chassagne as they often offer a very lively and vibrant expression of pinot noir.  Often have I been drinking

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Sure but if it was known for red they’d have some GCs.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:

Sure but if it was known for red they’d have some GCs.

Like NSG or Volnay?

I think the reason Chassagne was switched to whites was because of phylloxera and what the negotiants at the time wanted. I’m sure @William_Kelley would know a lot more about it though.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: More recent than that, in fact. It was a trend before, but it really accelerated when the US market for white Burgundy exploded in the 1980s. Chardonnay can produce higher yields than Pinot Noir, be bottled earlier, and by the 1980s it commanded a higher price… so the writing was on the wall for red Chassagne.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

More recent than that, in fact. It was a trend before, but it really accelerated when the US market for white Burgundy exploded in the 1980s. Chardonnay can produce higher yields than Pinot Noir, be bottled earlier, and by the 1980s it commanded a higher price… so the writing was on the wall for red Chassagne.

Too bad that there are not two Chassagnes. The whites from there can be fabulous, but I also love some of the better reds from there. The reds have been great values, but that is starting to change, unfortunately.

Younger people might not know what a big Chardonnay craze there was in the 1980s in the US. White wines (esp. Chardonnay but also including “white” Zinfandel) was replacing mixed drinks at cocktail parties as the drink of choice for many “yuppies” in the US, esp. for women. Kind of became the new daiquiris and pina coladas. Chardonnay in the US started being grown in a lot of places not really suited for it and wineries started using lots of new oak and residual sugar to make the wines more palatable. The one thing good about this era was the creation of white Zinfandel as a class saved a lot of really great old zinfandel vineyards when the market for Zinfandels crashed after the excesses in making Zins with higher and higher alcohol (probably only the older among us remember the craze for late harvest Amador Zinfandels).

The US market for white Burgundy increased in the 1980s as part of the same phenomenon. Generally, this was for those drinking at a higher level, but not necessarily when I think of some of the insipid wines from the Macon being sold here at that time.
